infobox.value: | | --- | | * [History](/wiki/History_of_atheism "History of atheism") * [Demographics](/wiki/Demographics_of_atheism "Demographics of atheism") * [Discrimination/Persecution](/wiki/Discrimination_against_atheists "Discrimination against atheists") * [Criticism](/wiki/Criticism_of_atheism "Criticism of atheism") * [Lists of atheists](/wiki/Lists_of_atheists "Lists of atheists") | | Forms and variations | | * [Implicit and explicit](/wiki/Implicit_and_explicit_atheism "Implicit and explicit atheism") * [Negative and positive](/wiki/Negative_and_positive_atheism "Negative and positive atheism") * [New](/wiki/New_Atheism "New Atheism") * [State](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") | | Aspects | | * [Antitheism](/wiki/Antitheism "Antitheism")   * [Relationship between atheism and religion](/wiki/Atheism_and_religion "Atheism and religion") * [Existence of God](/wiki/Existence_of_God "Existence of God") |

is the absence or rejection of [religious](/wiki/Religious "Religious") [beliefs](/wiki/Belief "Belief") or [practices](/wiki/Spiritual_practice "Spiritual practice"). It encompasses a wide range of viewpoints drawn from various [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophical "Philosophical") and [intellectual](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual") perspectives, including [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"), [agnosticism](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism"), [religious skepticism](/wiki/Religious_skepticism "Religious skepticism"), [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"), [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"), and [non-religious spirituality](/wiki/Spiritual_but_not_religious "Spiritual but not religious"). These perspectives can vary, with individuals who identify as irreligious holding diverse beliefs about religion and its role in their lives.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)

**Irreligion** is the absence or rejection of [religious](/wiki/Religious "Religious") [beliefs](/wiki/Belief "Belief") or [practices](/wiki/Spiritual_practice "Spiritual practice"). It encompasses a wide range of viewpoints drawn from various [philosophical](/wiki/Philosophical "Philosophical") and [intellectual](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual") perspectives, including [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"), [agnosticism](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism"), [religious skepticism](/wiki/Religious_skepticism "Religious skepticism"), [rationalism](/wiki/Rationalism "Rationalism"), [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism"), and [non-religious spirituality](/wiki/Spiritual_but_not_religious "Spiritual but not religious"). These perspectives can vary, with individuals who identify as irreligious holding diverse beliefs about religion and its role in their lives.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)

Relatively little scholarly research was published on irreligion until around the year 2010.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

Over the past several decades,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] the number of secular people has increased, with a rapid rise in the early 21st century, in many countries.[[3]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasquale2016-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 4[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112[[6]](#cite_note-InglehartWorldValuesSurvey2021-6) In virtually every [high-income country](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy") and many [poor countries](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries"), religion has declined.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 Highly secular societies tend to be societally healthy and successful.[[7]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016Conclusion-7) Social scientists have predicted declines in religious beliefs and their replacement with more scientific/naturalistic outlooks (secularization hypothesis).[[8]](#cite_note-EvolutionaryPsychologicalScience2017-8) According to [Ronald Inglehart](/wiki/Ronald_Inglehart "Ronald Inglehart"), this trend seems likely to continue and a reverse rarely lasts long because the trend is driven by [technological innovation](/wiki/Technological_innovation "Technological innovation").[[9]](#cite_note-9) However, other researchers disagree (contra-secularization hypothesis).[[8]](#cite_note-EvolutionaryPsychologicalScience2017-8) By 2050, [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") (Pew) expects irreligious people to probably decline as a share of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population") (16.4% to 13.2%), at least for a time, because of faster population growth in highly religious countries and [shrinking populations](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") in at least some less religious countries.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[10]](#cite_note-Pew2022-10) Many countries may also be gradually becoming more secular, generation by generation.[[10]](#cite_note-Pew2022-10) Younger generations tend to be less religious than their elders.[[10]](#cite_note-Pew2022-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Pew2018pdf-11): 5 They might become more religious as they age, but still be less religious than previous generations if their countries become more affluent and stable.[[11]](#cite_note-Pew2018pdf-11): 13 Nonetheless, secularization is compatible with religion since most versions of secularity do not lead to atheism or irreligion.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Religious congruence, that is consistency between beliefs and behaviors, in individuals is rare.[[13]](#cite_note-Chaves2010-13): 2 Religious incongruence is not the same thing as religious insincerity or hypocrisy.[[13]](#cite_note-Chaves2010-13): 5 The widespread religious congruence fallacy occurs when interpretations or explanations unjustifiably presume religious congruence.[[13]](#cite_note-Chaves2010-13): 19 This fallacy also infects "New Atheist" critiques of religion.[[13]](#cite_note-Chaves2010-13): 21

Estimating the number of irreligious people in the world is difficult.[[14]](#cite_note-AtheismThroughouttheWorld-14)[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1) Those who do not affiliate with a religion are diverse. In many countries censuses and demographic surveys do not separate atheists, agnostics and those responding "nothing in particular" as distinct populations, obscuring significant differences that may exist between them.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 60 People can feel reasonable anxieties about giving a politically ‘wrong’ answer – in either direction.[[14]](#cite_note-AtheismThroughouttheWorld-14) Measurement of irreligiosity requires a high degree of cultural sensitivity, especially outside the West, where the concepts of "religion" or "the secular" are not always rooted in local culture and may not even be present.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 31–34 The sharp distinction, and often antagonism, between "religious" and "secular" is culturally and historically unique to the West since in most of human history and cultures, there was little differentiation between the natural and supernatural and concepts do not always transfer across cultures.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 31 Forms of secularity always reflect the societal, historical, cultural and religious contexts in which they emerge, and distinctions are sharp in religiously dominant contexts.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 31 Also, there's considerable prevalence of atheism and agnosticism in ancient Asian texts.[[16]](#cite_note-SigneCohen-16) Atheistic traditions [have played a significant part in those cultures](/wiki/Nontheistic_religion "Nontheistic religion") for millennia.[[16]](#cite_note-SigneCohen-16) "Cultural religion" must be taken into account: non-religious people can be found in religious categories, especially where religion has very deep-seated religious roots in a culture.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59 Many of the religiously unaffiliated have some religious beliefs and participate in religious practices.[[17]](#cite_note-glo-17)[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[19]](#cite_note-East-19)[[20]](#cite_note-asi-20)

In 2016, [Zuckerman](/wiki/Phil_Zuckerman "Phil Zuckerman"), Galen and Pasquale estimated there were 400 million nonreligious or nontheistic people.[[21]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasquale2016chapter2-21)
A 2022 [Gallup International Association](/wiki/Gallup_International_Association "Gallup International Association") (GIA) survey, done in 61 countries, reported that 62% of respondents said they are religious, one in four that they aren't, 10% that they're atheists and the rest are not sure.[[22]](#cite_note-Gallup2022-22) In 2016, it found similar results (62%, 25%, 9% and 5%), also in 2014.[[22]](#cite_note-Gallup2022-22)[[23]](#cite_note-WIN/GallupInternational2016-23): 1: 3 People in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") were the least religious.[[22]](#cite_note-Gallup2022-22)
In 2010, according to Pew, the religiously unaffiliated numbered more than 1.1 billion, about one-in-six people (16.3% of an estimated 6.9 billion).[[24]](#cite_note-Pew_Global-24)[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 24: 25 76% of them resided in the 60 countries of [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia")-[Pacific](/wiki/Pacific "Pacific").[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 25: 46: 66 [China](/wiki/China "China"), officially an [atheist state](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") and considered to be the [world's first or second most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_(United_Nations) "List of countries by population (United Nations)"),[[note 1]](#cite_note-26) alone held the majority (62.2% or about 700 million).[[26]](#cite_note-Kuo2017OxfordAcademic-27)[[27]](#cite_note-Kuo2017-28): 1[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 25: 46: 66 Shares were relatively similar in three of the six regions: Asia-Pacific (21.2% of more than 4 billion), [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") (18.2% of more than 742 thousands) and [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") (17.1% of more than 344 thousands).[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 25 [Men](/wiki/Men "Men"), [younger people](/wiki/Youth "Youth"), and [whites](/wiki/Whites "Whites"), [Asians](/wiki/Asian_people "Asian people"), and people of [Jewish heritage](/wiki/Jewish_heritage "Jewish heritage") are more likely to be secular.[[7]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016Conclusion-7)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Etymology")]

Irreligion is either a borrowing from French or from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin").[[28]](#cite_note-OED-29) The term *irreligion* is a combination of the noun *religion* and the *ir-* form of the prefix *in-*, signifying "not" (similar to *irrelevant*). It was first attested in French as *irréligion* in 1527, then in English as *irreligion* in 1598. It was borrowed into [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language") as *irreligie* in the 17th century, though it is not certain from which language.[[29]](#cite_note-INT-30)

## Definition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Definition")]

According to the [encyclopedia](/wiki/Encyclopedia "Encyclopedia") *[Britannica](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica "Encyclopædia Britannica")*, the term irreligion is frequently characterized differently depending on context.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1) Sometimes, surveys of religious belief use lack of identification with a [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion") as a marker of irreligion.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1) This can be misleading: in some cases a person may identify with a religious cultural institution but not hold the doctrines of that institution or take part in its religious practice.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)

Some scholars define irreligion as the active rejection of religion, as opposed to the mere absence of religion.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1) The *Encyclopedia of Religion and Society* defines it as: "Active rejection of religion in general or any of its more specific organized forms. It is thus distinct from the secular, which simply refers to the absence of religion. [...] In contemporary usage, it is increasingly employed as a synonym for unbelief [...]"[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) Sociologist Colin Campbell also describes it as "deliberate indifference towards religion", in his 1971 *Towards a Sociology of Irreligion*.[[32]](#cite_note-33)

The *[Oxford English Dictionary](/wiki/Oxford_English_Dictionary "Oxford English Dictionary")* has two definitions, one of which is labelled [obsolete](/wiki/Obsolescence "Obsolescence") (first published in 1900).[[28]](#cite_note-OED-29) It is want of religion; hostility to or disregard of religious principles; irreligious conduct.[[28]](#cite_note-OED-29)

The *[Merriam Webster Dictionary](/wiki/Merriam_Webster_Dictionary "Merriam Webster Dictionary")* defines it as "the quality or state of being irreligious" and "irreligious" as "neglectful of religion: lacking religious emotions, doctrines, or practices", also "indicating lack of religion".[[33]](#cite_note-34)

Also for "religion", there is no universally agreed-upon definition, even within the social sciences.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 15

## Types

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Types")]

* [Agnostic atheism](/wiki/Agnostic_atheism "Agnostic atheism") is a philosophical position that encompasses both [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") and [agnosticism](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism"). Agnostic atheists are *atheistic* because they do not believe in the existence of any deity and *agnostic* because they claim that the existence of a [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity") is either unknowable in principle or unknown in fact.
* [Agnosticism](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism") is the view that [the existence of God](/wiki/The_existence_of_God "The existence of God"), the [divine](/wiki/Divinity "Divinity"), and the [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural") are unknown or [unknowable](/wiki/Uncertainty "Uncertainty").
* [Alatrism](/w/index.php?title=Alatrism&action=edit&redlink=1 "Alatrism (page does not exist)") or alatry ([Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"): from the privative *ἀ*- + *λατρεία (latreia)* = worship) is the recognition of the existence of one or more gods, but with a deliberate lack of worship of any deity. Typically, it includes the belief that religious rituals have no supernatural significance and that gods ignore all prayers and worship.
* [Anti-clericalism](/wiki/Anti-clericalism "Anti-clericalism") is opposition to religious authority, typically in social or political matters.
* [Antireligion](/wiki/Antireligion "Antireligion") is opposition to or rejection of religion of any kind.
* [Antitheism](/wiki/Antitheism "Antitheism") is the explicit opposition to [theism](/wiki/Theism "Theism"). The term has had a range of applications. It typically refers to direct opposition to belief in any [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity").
* [Apatheism](/wiki/Apatheism "Apatheism") is the attitude of [apathy](/wiki/Apathy "Apathy") or indifference toward the existence or non-existence of any deity.
* [Atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") is the lack of belief that any deities exist; in a narrower sense, positive atheism is specifically the position that there are factually no deities. There are ranges of [negative and positive atheism](/wiki/Negative_and_positive_atheism "Negative and positive atheism").
* "Cultural religion"[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59
* [Deism](/wiki/Deism "Deism") is a [philosophical position](/wiki/Philosophy "Philosophy") and [rationalistic theology](/wiki/Natural_theology "Natural theology") that rejects [revelation](/wiki/Revelation "Revelation") as a source of knowledge and asserts that [empirical](/wiki/Empirical_evidence "Empirical evidence") [reason](/wiki/Reason "Reason") and [observation](/wiki/Observation "Observation") of the [natural world](/wiki/Nature "Nature") are exclusively logical, reliable, and sufficient to determine the existence of a [Supreme Being](/wiki/God "God") as the [creator of the universe](/wiki/Creator_deity "Creator deity").
* [Freethought](/wiki/Freethought "Freethought").[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 14 It holds that positions regarding truth should be formed on the basis of logic, reason, and empiricism rather than authority, tradition, revelation, or [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma").
* [Ietsism](/wiki/Ietsism "Ietsism") is an unspecified belief in an undetermined [transcendent](/wiki/Transcendence_(religion) "Transcendence (religion)") reality.
* [Ignosticism](/wiki/Ignosticism "Ignosticism"), also known as *igtheism*, is the idea that the question of the [existence of God](/wiki/Existence_of_God "Existence of God") is meaningless because the word "God" has no coherent, unambiguous definition.
* [Naturalism](/wiki/Naturalism_(philosophy) "Naturalism (philosophy)") is the idea or belief that only natural (as opposed to supernatural or spiritual) laws and forces operate in the universe.
* [New Atheism](/wiki/New_Atheism "New Atheism") is the position of some atheist academics, writers, scientists, and philosophers of the 20th and 21st centuries, such as [Richard Dawkins](/wiki/Richard_Dawkins "Richard Dawkins"), [Sam Harris](/wiki/Sam_Harris "Sam Harris"), and [Daniel Dennett](/wiki/Daniel_Dennett "Daniel Dennett").
* Nones can be used to refer to those who are unaffiliated with any organized religion. This use derives from surveys of religious affiliation, in which "None" (or "None of the above") is typically the last choice. Since this status can be chosen because of lack of organizational affiliation or lack of personal belief, it is a more specific concept than irreligion. A 2015 [Gallup, Inc.](/wiki/Gallup,_Inc. "Gallup, Inc.") poll concluded that in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") "nones" were growing as a percentage of the population, while Christians were declining and non-Christians also increasing but to a much lesser degree, since the 1950s.[[34]](#cite_note-gallup-35)
* [Nontheism](/wiki/Nontheism "Nontheism")[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 14
* [Post-theism](/wiki/Post-theism "Post-theism") is a variant of [nontheism](/wiki/Nontheism "Nontheism") that proposes that the division of [theism](/wiki/Theism "Theism") and [atheism](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") is obsolete and that the God-idea belongs to a stage of human development now past. Within nontheism, post-theism can be contrasted with [antitheism](/wiki/Antitheism "Antitheism").
* [Religious skepticism](/wiki/Religious_skepticism "Religious skepticism") is a type of skepticism about religion.
* [Secular ethics](/wiki/Secular_ethics "Secular ethics") is a branch of moral philosophy in which ethics is based solely on human faculties, such as logic, empathy, reason, and ethical intuition, and not derived from belief in supernatural revelation or guidance—a source of ethics in many religions.
* [Secular humanism](/wiki/Secular_humanism "Secular humanism") is a system of thought that prioritizes human rather than divine matters.
* [Secular liberalism](/wiki/Secular_liberalism "Secular liberalism") is a form of [liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") in which secularist principles and values, and sometimes non-religious ethics, are especially emphasized.
* [Secular paganism](/wiki/Secular_paganism "Secular paganism") is an outlook that upholds the virtues and principles associated with [paganism](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") while maintaining a secular worldview.
* [Secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism").[[3]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasquale2016-3)[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 14 It is also used to describe a political conviction in favor of minimizing religion in the public sphere that may be advocated for regardless of personal [religiosity](/wiki/Religiosity "Religiosity"). Sometimes, especially in the United States, it is also a synonym for naturalism or atheism.[[35]](#cite_note-36)
* "[Spiritual but not religious](/wiki/Spiritual_but_not_religious "Spiritual but not religious")" (SBNR) is a designation coined by [Robert C. Fuller](/wiki/Robert_C._Fuller "Robert C. Fuller") for people who reject traditional or [organized religion](/wiki/Organized_religion "Organized religion") but have strong metaphysical beliefs. The SBNR may be included under the definition of nonreligion,[[36]](#cite_note-37) but are sometimes classified as a wholly distinct group.[[37]](#cite_note-38)
* [Theological noncognitivism](/wiki/Theological_noncognitivism "Theological noncognitivism") is the argument that religious language—specifically, words such as *God*—are not cognitively meaningful. It is sometimes considered synonymous with [ignosticism](/wiki/Ignosticism "Ignosticism").
* [Transtheism](/wiki/Transtheism "Transtheism") refers to a system of thought or [religious philosophy](/wiki/Religious_philosophy "Religious philosophy") that is neither [theistic](/wiki/Theism "Theism") nor [atheistic](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism") but beyond them.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

In the early 1970s, Colin Campbell began a sociological study of irreligion.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 13

## Human rights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Human rights")]

Main article: [Freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion")

In 1993, the [United Nations Human Rights Committee](/wiki/United_Nations_Human_Rights_Committee "United Nations Human Rights Committee") declared that article 18 of the [International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights](/wiki/International_Covenant_on_Civil_and_Political_Rights "International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights") "protects theistic, non-theistic and atheistic beliefs, as well as the right not to profess any religion or belief."[[38]](#cite_note-39) The committee further stated that "the freedom to have or to adopt a religion or belief necessarily entails the freedom to choose a religion or belief, including the right to replace one's current religion or belief with another or to adopt atheistic views." Signatories to the convention are barred from "the use of threat of physical force or penal sanctions to compel believers or non-believers" to recant their beliefs or convert.[[39]](#cite_note-fdih1-40)[[40]](#cite_note-41)

Most [democracies](/wiki/Democracies "Democracies") protect the [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") or belief, and it is largely implied in respective [legal systems](/wiki/Legal_systems "Legal systems") that those who do not believe or observe any religion are allowed [freedom of thought](/wiki/Freedom_of_thought "Freedom of thought").

A noted exception to ambiguity, explicitly allowing non-religion, is Article 36 of the [Constitution of China](/wiki/Constitution_of_China "Constitution of China") (as adopted in 1982), which states that "No state organ, public organization or individual may compel citizens to believe in, or not believe in, any religion; nor may they discriminate against citizens who believe in, or do not believe in, any religion."[[41]](#cite_note-42) Article 46 of China's [1978 Constitution](/wiki/1978_Constitution_of_the_People%27s_Republic_of_China "1978 Constitution of the People's Republic of China") was even more explicit, stating that "Citizens enjoy freedom to believe in religion and freedom not to believe in religion and to propagate atheism."[[42]](#cite_note-43)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main article: [List of countries by irreligion](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_irreligion "List of countries by irreligion")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Countries_by_percentage_of_Unaffiliated%E2%80%93Pew_Research_2010.svg/960px-Countries_by_percentage_of_Unaffiliated%E2%80%93Pew_Research_2010.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Countries_by_percentage_of_Unaffiliated%E2%80%93Pew_Research_2010.svg)

[Nonreligious population by country](/wiki/Irreligion_by_country "Irreligion by country"), in 2010[[43]](#cite_note-44)

Women, when in the [labor force](/wiki/Labor_force "Labor force"), are similar to men in their [religiosity](/wiki/Religiosity "Religiosity"). When out of the labor force, women tend to be more religious.[[44]](#cite_note-NationalGeographic2016-45)

In many countries censuses and demographic surveys do not separate atheists, agnostics and those responding "nothing in particular" as distinct populations.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 60 Both "religion" and "secular" are Western concepts and are not universal across cultures, languages, or time.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

In 2020, of the global atheist and non-religious population, 78% live in Asia and the Pacific, while the remainder reside in Europe (10%), North America (6%), Latin America and the Caribbean (4%), sub-Saharan Africa (1.5%) and the Middle East and North Africa (.1%).[[46]](#cite_note-47) Eleven countries have nonreligious majorities. In 2020, the countries with the highest percentage of "Non-Religious" ("Term encompassing both (a) agnostics; and (b) atheists") were [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea "North Korea"), the [Czech Republic](/wiki/Czech_Republic "Czech Republic") and [Estonia](/wiki/Estonia "Estonia").[[47]](#cite_note-48) According to the 2018 Chinese General Social Survey, China had the largest count of unaffiliated people: about one billion adults.[[48]](#cite_note-PewAugust2023-49) According to Pew Research in 2025, China alone makes up 67% of the global religiously unaffiliated demographic.[[49]](#cite_note-unaf_2025-50) Some broadly religious practices continue to play a significant role in the lives of a substantial shares of the Chinese population.[[48]](#cite_note-PewAugust2023-49) The Asia-Pacific region alone accounts for 78% of the global unaffiliated demographic.[[49]](#cite_note-unaf_2025-50)

Determining objective irreligion, as part of societal or individual levels of [secularity](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") and religiosity, requires a high degree of [cultural sensitivity](/wiki/Cultural_sensitivity "Cultural sensitivity") from [researchers](/wiki/Researchers "Researchers"). This is especially so outside the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world"), where the concepts of "religious" and "secular" are not necessarily rooted in local [culture](/wiki/Culture "Culture") or even exist.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 31–34 "Cultural religion" is a vivid reality.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59 It must be taken into account when trying to ascertain the numeric strength of atheism and agnosticism in a country.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59 It is generally not considered more important than self-identification measures.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59 Non-religious people can be found in religious categories.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59 And many of the unaffiliated still hold religious beliefs or practices.[[17]](#cite_note-glo-17)[[20]](#cite_note-asi-20)[[19]](#cite_note-East-19) This is especially the case where religion has very deep-seated religious roots in a culture, such as with Christianity in Europe, Islam in the Middle East, Hinduism in India, and Buddhism in South-east Asia.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 59 For instance, [Scandinavian](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") countries have among the highest measures of nonreligiosity and even atheism in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"). For example, 58% of the [Swedish population](/wiki/Demographics_of_Sweden "Demographics of Sweden") identify with the [Church of Sweden](/wiki/Church_of_Sweden "Church of Sweden").[[50]](#cite_note-51) Yet, 47% of atheists who live in those countries are still formally members of the national churches.[[51]](#cite_note-52) In much of [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), ritual behavior holds greater salience than belief.[[4]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasqualebook2016-4): 31 China has [state atheism](/wiki/State_atheism "State atheism") and is a Leninist religious state, which maintains dominance over all other religions.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[26]](#cite_note-Kuo2017OxfordAcademic-27)[[27]](#cite_note-Kuo2017-28): 1 About 85% of its population practice various kinds of religious behaviors with some regularity.[[27]](#cite_note-Kuo2017-28): 2 Many East Asians identify as "without religion" (*wú zōngjiào* in Chinese, *mu shūkyō* in Japanese, *mu jong-gyo* in [Korean](/wiki/Korean_language "Korean language")), but "religion" in that context refers only to [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") or Christianity. Most of the people "without religion" practice [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") and other [folk religions](/wiki/Folk_religion "Folk religion"). In the [Muslim world](/wiki/Muslim_world "Muslim world"), those who claim to be "not religious" mostly imply not strictly observing Islam, and in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), being "[secular](/wiki/Hiloni "Hiloni")" means not strictly observing [Orthodox Judaism](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism"). Vice versa, many [American Jews](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") share the worldviews of nonreligious people though affiliated with a Jewish denomination, and in [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), growing identification with [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") is mainly motivated by cultural and nationalist considerations, without much concrete belief.[[52]](#cite_note-53) In the United States, the majority of the "Nones", those without a religious affiliation, have belief in a god or higher power, spiritual forces beyond the natural world, and souls.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Even 23% of self-identified atheists believe in a [higher power](/wiki/Higher_power "Higher power"), but not a god as described in the bible.[[54]](#cite_note-Pew_2-55) A 2024 report by Pew on Hong Kong, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan and Vietnam found that though many in those countries are not affiliated with any religion, many of the unaffiliated still have beliefs in gods or unseen beings and engage in religious practices.[[19]](#cite_note-East-19) According to Pew in 2023, many unaffiliated Chinese believe in deities or other religious beliefs and participate in religious practicies.[[48]](#cite_note-PewAugust2023-49)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

Secular people are complex and not always devoid of religious or spiritual involvement.[[56]](#cite_note-57) They persist in various forms of religiosity and spirituality.[[57]](#cite_note-58) "Secular churches" or "atheist churches" such as [Sunday Assembly](/wiki/Sunday_Assembly "Sunday Assembly") and others have emerged in multiple countries (e.g. USA, Britain, Australia) which deal with community needs that religious services often provide.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62) Atheists are diverse with some engaging spiritual practice and others having strong beliefs in that they seek to evangelize to others.[[62]](#cite_note-63) [Nontheistic religions](/wiki/Nontheistic_religion "Nontheistic religion") such as Buddhism, Jainism, along with atheistic schools in Hinduism, and other Indian schools like Carvaka, Ajivika have played a significant part in Asian cultures for millennia.[[16]](#cite_note-SigneCohen-16) [Anthropologist](/wiki/Anthropologist "Anthropologist") Jack David Eller states that "atheism is quite a common position, even within religion" and that "surprisingly, atheism is not the opposite or lack, let alone the enemy, of religion but is the most common form of religion."[[63]](#cite_note-Eller-64) In the Czech Republic, one of the most secular countries in the world, most nonbelievers are not atheists but are more religious skeptics fulfill their spiritual needs outside of traditional religion.[[64]](#cite_note-65)

In 2014, [Zuckerman](/wiki/Phil_Zuckerman "Phil Zuckerman"), Galen and Pasquale estimated there were 400 million nonreligious or nontheistic people.[[21]](#cite_note-ZuckermanGalenPasquale2016chapter2-21) In their 2013 essay, Ariela Keysar and Juhem Navarro-Rivera estimated there were about 450 to 500 million nonbelievers, including both "positive" and "negative" atheists, or approximately 7% of the world population.[[65]](#cite_note-66) These estimates come from the [International Social Survey Programme](/wiki/International_Social_Survey_Programme "International Social Survey Programme") 2008 survey in which 40 countries took part.[[66]](#cite_note-67) In 2010, the religiously unaffiliated numbered more than 1.1 billion (around 1,126,500,000 persons), about one-in-six people (16.3% of an estimated 6,9 billion [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population")), according to [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center").[[24]](#cite_note-Pew_Global-24)[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 24: 25 In Pew reports, "unaffiliated" are atheists, agnostics, and people who checked "nothing in particular".[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15): 60 76% of them resided in one of the six regions: [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia")-Pacific.[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 25 A 2012 [WIN/Gallup International](/wiki/WIN/GIA "WIN/GIA") report on a poll from 57 countries reported that 59% of the world's population identified as a religious person, 23% as not a religious person, 13% as "convinced atheists", and also a 9% decrease in identification as "religious" when compared to the 2005 average from 39 countries.[[67]](#cite_note-GallupInt2012-68) A 2015 WIN/Gallup International poll found that 63% of the globe identified as a religious person, 22% as not a religious person, and 11% as "convinced atheists".[[68]](#cite_note-GallupInt2015-69) Their 2016 survey found that 62% of the globe identified as a religious person, less than 25% as not a religious person, 9% others as "convinced atheists" and 5% others "Do not know/no response".[[23]](#cite_note-WIN/GallupInternational2016-23) Keysar and Navarro-Rivera advised caution with these figures since other surveys have consistently reached lower figures for the number of atheists worldwide.[[69]](#cite_note-70): 553: 554

Inverse association between [intelligence and religiosity](/wiki/Religiosity_and_intelligence "Religiosity and intelligence"), and the inverse correlation between intelligence and [fertility](/wiki/Fertility "Fertility") might lead to a decline in non-religious identity (contra-secularization hypothesis) in the foreseeable future.[[8]](#cite_note-EvolutionaryPsychologicalScience2017-8)[[70]](#cite_note-71): 2 In 2007, sociologist [Phil Zuckerman](/wiki/Phil_Zuckerman "Phil Zuckerman")'s global studies on atheism have indicated that global atheism may be in decline due to irreligious countries having the lowest birth rates in the world and religious countries having higher birth rates in general.[[71]](#cite_note-zucker-72) A Pew 2015 global projection study for religion and nonreligion, projected that between 2010 and 2050, there will be some initial increases of the unaffiliated followed by a decline by 2050.[[72]](#cite_note-73) Some theorists think religion will fade away but Pew reveals a more complicated picture.[[10]](#cite_note-Pew2022-10) Pew predicts the unaffiliated share of the [world population](/wiki/World_population "World population") will decrease, at least for a while, from 16.4% to 13.2% by 2050.[[73]](#cite_note-Pew2015-74)[[10]](#cite_note-Pew2022-10) Pew states that religious areas are experiencing the fastest growth because of higher fertility and younger populations.[[10]](#cite_note-Pew2022-10)[[74]](#cite_note-75) By 2060, Pew says the number of unaffiliated will increase by over 35 million, but the overall population-percentage will decrease to 13% because the total population will grow faster.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77) This would be mostly because of relatively [old age](/wiki/Population_ageing "Population ageing") and [low fertility rates](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") in less religious societies such as [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), particularly [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), but also [Western Europe](/wiki/Western_Europe "Western Europe").[[73]](#cite_note-Pew2015-74)[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1) By 2019, 43 out of 49 countries studied continued to become less religious.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 110[[6]](#cite_note-InglehartWorldValuesSurvey2021-6)

Relatively few unbelievers select ‘Atheist’ or ‘Agnostic’ as their preferred (non)religious or secular identity.[[77]](#cite_note-78): 3 Being nonreligious is not necessarily equivalent to being an atheist or agnostic. Many of the nonreligious have some religious beliefs.[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 24 Also, some of the unaffiliated engage in certain kinds of religious practices.[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 24 For example, "belief in God or a higher power is shared by 7% of Chinese unaffiliated adults, 30% of French unaffiliated adults and 68% of unaffiliated U.S. adults.[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 24 Being unaffiliated with a religion on polls does not automatically mean objectively nonreligious since there are, for example, unaffiliated people who fall under religious measures, just as some unbelievers may still attend a church or other place of worship.[[15]](#cite_note-JohnsonZurlo2016-15)[*[pages needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] Out of the global nonreligious population, 76.2% reside in Asia-Pacific, while the remainder reside in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") (12%), [North America](/wiki/North_America "North America") (5.2%), [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") (4%), [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa") (2.4%) and the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") (0.2%).[[18]](#cite_note-Pew2012-18)[[25]](#cite_note-Pew2012pdf-25): 24

### 2020 Population Estimate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2020 Population Estimate")]

Irreligious individuals constitute 24.2% of the global population, with a total population of 1,905,360,000. 88.5% of this irreligious population is concentrated in ten countries, ranked by the size of their respective populations: [China](/wiki/China "China"), the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"), [France](/wiki/France "France"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea"). China hosts more than 1,270,000,000; the United States more than 100,000,000; Japan more than 70,000,000; Vietnam more than 60,000,000; Germany more than 30,000,000; and Russia, Brazil, France, the United Kingdom, and South Korea each contain more than 20,000,000 irreligious individuals.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

## Historical trends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Historical trends")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Abandoned_uniting_church.jpg/250px-Abandoned_uniting_church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abandoned_uniting_church.jpg)

An abandoned church in Australia

Since 2007, there has been a sharp trend away from religion.[[6]](#cite_note-InglehartWorldValuesSurvey2021-6)[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5) From about 2007 to 2019, 43 out of 49 countries studied became less religious.[[6]](#cite_note-InglehartWorldValuesSurvey2021-6)
Past influential thinkers from [Karl Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") to [Max Weber](/wiki/Max_Weber "Max Weber") to [Émile Durkheim](/wiki/%C3%89mile_Durkheim "Émile Durkheim") thought that the spread of scientific knowledge would dispel religion throughout the world.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 Industrialization also didn't cause religion to disappear.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 110 [Political scientists](/wiki/Political_science "Political science") [Ronald Inglehart](/wiki/Ronald_Inglehart "Ronald Inglehart") and [Pippa Norris](/wiki/Pippa_Norris "Pippa Norris") argue faith is "more emotional than cognitive", and both advance an alternative thesis termed "existential security." They postulate that rather than knowledge or ignorance of scientific learning, it is the weakness or vulnerability of a society that determines religiosity. They claim that increased poverty and chaos make religious values more important to a society, while wealth and security diminish its role. As need for religious support diminishes, there is less willingness to "accept its constraints, including keeping women in the kitchen and gay people in the closet".[[79]](#cite_note-Ikenberry-review-2004-80)

### Prior to the 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Prior to the 1980s")]

Rates of people identifying as non-religious began rising in most societies at least as early as the turn of the 20th century.[[80]](#cite_note-nones-81) In 1968, sociologist Glenn M. Vernon wrote that [US census](/wiki/United_States_census "United States census") respondents who identified as "no religion" were insufficiently defined because they were [defined in terms of a negative](/wiki/Definition#problems "Definition"). He contrasted the label with the term "independent" for political affiliation, which still includes people who participate in [civic activities](/wiki/Civic_engagement "Civic engagement"). He suggested this difficulty in definition was partially due to the dilemma of defining religious activity beyond membership, attendance, or other identification with a formal religious group.[[80]](#cite_note-nones-81) During the 1970s, social scientists still tended to describe irreligion from a perspective that considered religion as normative for humans. Irreligion was described in terms of hostility, reactivity, or indifference toward religion or as developing from radical theologies.[[81]](#cite_note-schumaker-82)

### 1981–2019

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Irreligion&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1981–2019")]

In a study of religious trends in 49 countries (they contained 60 percent of the world's population) from 1981 to 2007, Inglehart and Norris found an overall, but not universal, increase in religiosity.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 110 Respondents in 33 of 49 countries rated themselves higher on a scale from one to ten when asked how important God was in their lives. This increase occurred in most former communist and developing countries. Most high-income countries became less religious.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 A sharp reversal of the global trend occurred from 2007 to 2019, when 43 out of 49 countries studied became less religious. This reversal appeared across most of the world.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5) The decline in belief was not confined to [high-income countries](/wiki/World_Bank_high-income_economy "World Bank high-income economy") and appeared across most of the [world](/wiki/Earth "Earth").[[6]](#cite_note-InglehartWorldValuesSurvey2021-6) In virtually every high-income country, religion has continued to decline.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 At the same time, many [poor countries](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries"), together with most of the former communist states, have also become less religious.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 From 2007 to 2019, only five countries became more religious, whereas the vast majority of the countries studied moved in the opposite direction.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 India is the most important exception to the general pattern of declining [religiosity](/wiki/Religiosity "Religiosity").[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5): 112 The United States was a dramatic example of declining religiosity – with the mean rating of importance of religion dropping from 8.2 to 4.6 – while India was a major exception. Research in 1989 recorded disparities in religious adherence for different faith groups, with people from Christian and tribal traditions leaving religion at a greater rate than those from Muslim, Hindu, or Buddhist faiths.[[82]](#cite_note-83)

Inglehart and Norris speculate that the decline in religiosity comes from a decline in the social need for traditional [gender](/wiki/Gender "Gender") and [sexual norms](/wiki/Sexual_norms "Sexual norms"), ("virtually all world religions instilled" pro-fertility norms such as "producing as many children as possible and discouraged [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"), [abortion](/wiki/Abortion "Abortion"), [homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality"), [contraception](/wiki/Contraception "Contraception"), and any [sexual behavior](/wiki/Sexual_behavior "Sexual behavior") not linked to [reproduction](/wiki/Reproduction "Reproduction")" in their adherents for centuries) as [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") rose and [infant mortality](/wiki/Infant_mortality "Infant mortality") dropped. They also argue that the idea that religion was necessary to prevent a collapse of [social cohesion](/wiki/Social_cohesion "Social cohesion") and [public morality](/wiki/Public_morality "Public morality") was belied by lower levels of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption "Corruption") and [murder](/wiki/Murder "Murder") in less religious countries. They argue that both of these trends are based on the theory that as societies develop, survival becomes more secure: [starvation](/wiki/Starvation "Starvation"), once pervasive, becomes uncommon; life expectancy increases; murder and other forms of [violence](/wiki/Violence "Violence") diminish. As this level of security rises, there is less social/economic need for the high birthrates that religion encourages and less emotional need for the comfort of religious belief.[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5) Change in acceptance of "divorce, abortion, and homosexuality" has been measured by the [World Values Survey](/wiki/World_Values_Survey "World Values Survey") and shown to have grown throughout the world outside of [Muslim-majority countries](/wiki/Muslim-majority_countries "Muslim-majority countries").[[5]](#cite_note-InglehartForeignAffairs2020-5) Several very comprehensive surveys in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East") and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran") have come to similar conclusions: there is an increase in secularization and growing calls for reforms in religious political institutions.[[83]](#cite_note-84)
